questions.question: According to attribution theory, what is the most adaptive attribution pattern for failure?

options.text: Internal + Stable + Global: 'I failed because I'm inherently incompetent at everything'
Internal + Unstable + Specific: 'I failed because of my strategy (internal), which I can change (unstable), on this particular task (specific)'
External + Stable + Global: 'I failed because the world is against me in all ways'
Never analyze failures—just move on immediately

questions.explanation: The most adaptive failure attribution is Internal + Unstable + Specific: attribute failure to changeable factors you control (internal), that can be modified (unstable), in specific domains (specific). Example: 'I failed because I used the wrong approach (changeable) on this project (specific).' This preserves agency (internal) without hopelessness (unstable = can change) or catastrophizing (specific = doesn't generalize). The maladaptive pattern is Internal + Stable + Global ('I'm inherently incompetent at everything')—this creates hopelessness. Growth mindset lives in unstable attributions.

questions.question: What are the protective factors after failure according to systematic review research?

options.text: Higher self-esteem, constructive attributional style, and lower socially-prescribed perfectionism—all supporting learning orientation over rumination

questions.explanation: Systematic review of resilience to failure found strongest protective factors: (1) Higher self-esteem (stable self-worth despite failure), (2) Constructive attributional style (analyze controllable factors without catastrophizing), (3) Lower socially-prescribed perfectionism (failure doesn't equal worthlessness). These all support learning orientation (extract lessons, adjust strategy) over rumination (replay shame) or avoidance (not processing). Self-compassion (56+ RCTs) is the most evidence-backed skill for this—more effective than self-esteem enhancement alone.

questions.question: How does learning-oriented reflection differ from rumination after failure?

options.text: Learning is forward-focused, specific, and time-limited ('What will I do differently?'); rumination is backward-focused, global, and circular ('Why did this happen to me?')
Rumination is more helpful for growth
Learning requires no reflection—just immediate action

questions.explanation: Critical distinction: Learning-oriented reflection is forward-focused ('What will I do differently next time?'), specific ('This particular strategy didn't work'), problem-solving ('How can I improve?'), and time-limited (process, learn, move forward). Rumination is backward-focused ('Why did this happen to me?'), global ('I always fail,' 'I'm terrible at everything'), blame-focused ('What's wrong with me?'), and circular (endless replaying without resolution). Rumination maintains distress and predicts depression/anxiety. Learning extracts actionable insights.

questions.question: Why is self-compassion (56+ RCTs) considered the most well-evidenced approach for post-failure wellbeing?

options.text: Because it makes you stop caring about failure
Because it treats yourself kindly during difficulty (vs. self-criticism), validates suffering without catastrophizing, and supports learning without shame—more effective than self-esteem enhancement
Because it eliminates all negative emotions

questions.explanation: Self-compassion (meta-analysis of 56 RCTs) shows small-to-medium effects on depression, anxiety, and stress reduction—strongest evidence for post-failure resilience. Why: (1) Self-kindness allows acknowledging mistakes without shame (vs. self-criticism which blocks learning), (2) Common humanity normalizes struggle (vs. isolation: 'only I fail'), (3) Mindfulness holds pain without rumination or suppression. Research shows self-compassion is MORE effective than self-esteem enhancement for failure recovery because it's unconditional (doesn't depend on success). Self-compassion supports both emotional processing AND strategic learning.

questions.question: Apply the 7-step post-failure protocol to a recent setback: (1) Acknowledge failure and emotions, (2) Separate controllable from uncontrollable factors, (3) Extract specific learning, (4) Distinguish learning from rumination, (5) Make specific adjustment plan, (6) Re-engage with mastery goal, (7) Practice self-compassion throughout. What did you learn? What will you change?

title: Reframing Failure: From Threat to Learning - Quiz
